def: A distressing inner restlessness with an urge to move and inability to sit still; a common early antipsychotic side effect, linked to distress and suicidality.
Rhythmic involuntary shaking; a fine tremor is common with lithium and SSRIs, while a resting tremor points to drug-induced parkinsonism.
Excessive sweating; a feature of autonomic instability in serotonin syndrome, NMS, and alcohol or opioid withdrawal.
Exaggerated deep-tendon reflexes; together with clonus it is a hallmark of serotonin syndrome (contrast the hyporeflexia of NMS).
A drop in blood pressure on standing causing dizziness or fainting; common with alpha-1 blockade and low-potency antipsychotics.
Pupillary dilation; a sign of anticholinergic toxicity and of some drug overdoses.
Pupillary constriction; pinpoint pupils are a classic sign of opioid toxicity.
Abnormally slow heart rate; a risk with alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, dexmedetomidine) and cholinesterase inhibitors.
Abnormally fast heart rate; seen in anticholinergic toxicity, stimulant use, and withdrawal states.
Slowness of movement; a core feature of drug-induced parkinsonism from dopamine (D2) blockade.
Increased muscle tone; lead-pipe rigidity is a hallmark of NMS, while cogwheel rigidity marks drug-induced parkinsonism.
Sustained involuntary muscle contractions causing twisting or abnormal postures; acute dystonia is an early antipsychotic reaction.
An acute dystonic reaction with forced, sustained upward deviation of the eyes; treated urgently with anticholinergics.
Antipsychotic-induced bradykinesia, rigidity and resting tremor mimicking Parkinson disease, caused by dopamine (D2) blockade.
A lace-like, purplish mottling of the skin; a signature adverse effect of amantadine.
Tissue swelling from fluid; ankle edema is a tell of amantadine, and alpha-2-delta ligands (gabapentin, pregabalin) cause peripheral edema.
Sudden, brief involuntary muscle jerks; seen in serotonin syndrome and in moderate-to-severe lithium toxicity.
Rhythmic involuntary muscle contractions elicited on stretch; a key discriminating sign of serotonin syndrome.
Impaired coordination and unsteady gait; a feature of lithium toxicity and of benzodiazepine excess.
Slurred, poorly articulated speech from impaired motor control; appears in moderate-to-severe lithium toxicity.
Involuntary rhythmic eye movements; can accompany lithium toxicity and sedative intoxication.
A prolonged, often painful erection unrelated to arousal; a urologic emergency classically linked to trazodone (alpha-1 blockade).
Inappropriate breast-milk production; a sign of hyperprolactinemia from dopamine-blocking antipsychotics.
High prolactin from dopamine (D2) blockade, causing galactorrhea, menstrual changes and sexual dysfunction; most marked with risperidone and paliperidone.
Visible slowing of movement, speech and thought; a neurovegetative feature of depression (contrast psychomotor agitation).
Inability to form new memories after a given point; a benzodiazepine and hypnotic effect (e.g., triazolam, zopiclone, midazolam).
Abnormal tingling or pins-and-needles sensation; seen with topiramate and in antidepressant discontinuation.
Brief electric-shock-like sensations, often on head movement; a classic antidepressant discontinuation symptom.
Reddening and warmth of the skin; a hallmark of the disulfiram-alcohol reaction and of anticholinergic toxicity.
Inability to fully empty the bladder; an anticholinergic and noradrenergic (SNRI) adverse effect.
Impaired libido, arousal or orgasm; a common serotonergic (SSRI/clomipramine) side effect and often the main complaint.
A false sensory perception (often visual or auditory) occurring without a real stimulus; a core psychotic symptom.
A fixed false belief held despite clear contrary evidence; a core symptom of psychosis.
A sense of detachment from oneself (depersonalisation) or one's surroundings (derealisation); a ketamine effect and a PTSD feature.
A strong urge to use a substance; a target of relapse-prevention drugs such as naltrexone, acamprosate and varenicline.
A mood disorder of persistent low mood or loss of interest with neurovegetative changes in sleep, appetite, energy and concentration.
A period of abnormally elevated or irritable mood with increased energy, reduced need for sleep, grandiosity and impulsivity.
A milder form of mania: elevated or irritable mood with increased activity, but without psychosis or marked functional impairment.
A mood disorder with episodes of mania or hypomania and usually depression; bipolar depression is its depressive pole.
Loss of contact with reality — hallucinations, delusions and disorganized thinking — seen in schizophrenia and other disorders.
A chronic psychotic disorder with hallucinations, delusions, disorganization and negative symptoms.
An acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness from a medical cause; often worse in the evening and usually reversible.
A progressive, chronic decline in memory and cognition that impairs function; Alzheimer disease is the commonest cause.

aliases: behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia
def: Behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (agitation, aggression, psychosis); address unmet needs and triggers before reaching for drugs.
A syndrome of motor and behavioural dysregulation (immobility, mutism, posturing); a lorazepam challenge aids diagnosis and treatment.
Chronic, excessive and hard-to-control worry with physical tension, restlessness and sleep disturbance.
Recurrent unexpected panic attacks — sudden surges of intense fear with somatic symptoms and fear of dying or losing control.
Marked fear of scrutiny or negative evaluation in social or performance situations, leading to avoidance.
Intrusive, distressing obsessions and repetitive compulsions performed to relieve them; ritual relief is only fleeting.
Re-experiencing, avoidance, negative mood and hyperarousal persisting after a traumatic event.
Difficulty falling or staying asleep, or early-morning waking, despite adequate opportunity, with daytime consequences.
A chronic sleep disorder of excessive daytime sleepiness and sudden sleep attacks; treated with wakefulness agents like modafinil.
A chronic disorder of compulsive alcohol use despite harm; features craving, tolerance and a withdrawal syndrome on stopping.
A disorder of compulsive opioid use; overdose causes miosis, sedation and respiratory depression, the lethal feature.
Maintenance treatment of opioid use disorder with methadone or buprenorphine/naloxone to reduce craving and overdose risk.
Nicotine dependence from tobacco use; treated with varenicline, bupropion or nicotine replacement plus counselling.
Developmentally excessive inattention, hyperactivity and impulsivity with childhood onset and cross-situational impairment.
A behavioural emergency of escalating motor activity and arousal that risks harm; managed with de-escalation then medication.
Excess serotonergic activity causing a triad of neuromuscular excitation (clonus, hyperreflexia), autonomic instability and altered mental status.
A rare, life-threatening antipsychotic reaction: fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered mental status, autonomic instability and raised creatine kinase.
Drug-induced movement disorders from dopamine (D2) blockade: acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism and tardive dyskinesia.
Late-onset, often irreversible involuntary movements (especially orofacial) from chronic dopamine blockade; treated with VMAT2 inhibitors.
Flu-like symptoms, dizziness and brain zaps after abruptly stopping an antidepressant; worst with short-half-life SSRIs/SNRIs.
Lengthening of the ECG QT interval, raising the risk of torsades de pointes; a concern with many antipsychotics and methadone.
A polymorphic ventricular tachycardia triggered by a prolonged QT interval; can degenerate into fatal arrhythmia.
Low serum sodium; SSRIs/SNRIs can cause it via SIADH (especially in older women), producing confusion, unsteadiness and falls.
A dangerous fall in neutrophils leaving the patient prone to serious infection; the reason clozapine mandates regular blood counts.
Antipsychotic-driven weight gain, dyslipidemia and hyperglycemia raising cardiometabolic risk; worst with olanzapine and clozapine.
Toxicity above ~1.5 mmol/L: tremor and GI upset, then ataxia, confusion and seizures; precipitated by dehydration, NSAIDs and thiazides.
A hypertensive emergency when MAOIs meet dietary tyramine (aged cheese, cured meats, tap beer): throbbing headache and soaring blood pressure.
Antimuscarinic overload: dry flushed skin, dilated pupils, urinary retention, fever, tachycardia and delirium — worst in the elderly.
Severe, potentially fatal blistering mucocutaneous reactions; a lamotrigine and clobazam risk driven by rapid dose titration.
Inflammation of heart muscle; an early clozapine complication (fever, chest pain, rising troponin and CRP) in the first weeks of therapy.
A dangerous hyperglycemic emergency; can be precipitated by high-metabolic-risk antipsychotics such as olanzapine and clozapine.
Iris billowing during cataract surgery; flag alpha-1-blocker use (e.g., tamsulosin, prazosin) to the eye surgeon beforehand.
Passage of large volumes of dilute urine; lithium classically causes a nephrogenic form where the kidney stops responding to vasopressin.
Inflammation of the pancreas with severe abdominal pain; a recognized adverse effect of valproate.
A sudden rise in eye pressure causing a painful red eye and vision loss; topiramate and anticholinergics can precipitate it.
A surge in blood pressure after abruptly stopping a central alpha-2 agonist (clonidine, guanfacine); taper to avoid it.
Transiently worse insomnia after stopping a hypnotic; common with short-acting Z-drugs and benzodiazepines.
Breakdown of muscle releasing myoglobin that can injure the kidneys; a complication of NMS and severe serotonin syndrome.
A congenital malformation of the heart's tricuspid valve; a small first-trimester risk associated with lithium exposure.
Acute narrowing of the airways causing wheeze; non-selective beta-blockers and inhaled loxapine can trigger it in asthma or COPD.
Complex acts such as sleep-driving or sleep-eating performed while not fully awake, with no memory afterwards; a Z-drug and hypnotic risk.
Jaundice from impaired bile flow; a classic phenothiazine (e.g., chlorpromazine) adverse effect.
Heightened skin sensitivity to sunlight causing easy burning; a classic chlorpromazine and phenothiazine effect.
Drug-induced liver injury; naltrexone is dose-dependently hepatotoxic and disulfiram can be fulminant — monitor liver enzymes.
Dangerously slow, shallow breathing; the lethal feature of opioid and barbiturate overdose, worsened by combining CNS depressants.
Excess acid in the blood; topiramate's carbonic-anhydrase inhibition can cause it — check serum bicarbonate.
Abrupt, severe withdrawal when an antagonist or partial agonist displaces a full opioid; a risk when starting buprenorphine or giving naloxone.
The ease with which seizures are triggered; bupropion, clozapine, TCAs and maprotiline lower it, raising seizure risk.
A prolonged or repeated seizure without recovery in between — a neurological emergency; IV lorazepam is first-line.
The time for a drug's blood level to fall by half; longer half-lives allow less frequent dosing and gentler discontinuation.
Gradually adjusting a drug's dose up or down to balance efficacy against tolerability; slow titration can be a safety measure (e.g., lamotrigine).
An inactive drug converted in the body to its active form; e.g., lisdexamfetamine and clorazepate.
A pharmacologically active breakdown product of a drug that can prolong or add to its effects (e.g., nordiazepam from diazepam).
Metabolism of an oral drug by the gut and liver before it reaches the circulation, reducing how much active drug gets in.
A liver enzyme metabolizing many psychotropics; poor metabolizers or inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) raise substrate levels.
A major drug-metabolizing enzyme; inhibitors raise and inducers lower levels of its substrates (e.g., triazolam, lurasidone, pimozide).
A liver enzyme metabolizing clozapine and duloxetine; smoking induces it, while fluvoxamine strongly inhibits it, raising levels.
A liver enzyme metabolizing citalopram and escitalopram; poor metabolizers or inhibitors raise levels — a QT concern with citalopram.
The margin between an effective and a toxic dose; a narrow index (lithium, barbiturates) means small changes can cause toxicity.
A drug-free interval when switching medications to let the first clear and avoid interactions — e.g., before and after an MAOI.
A drug that binds a receptor but produces only a submaximal effect, buffering both under- and over-activity (aripiprazole, buprenorphine).
The blood concentration used to guide dosing, usually drawn as a trough (e.g., lithium 12-hour level) to keep it effective but non-toxic.
When a drug speeds up a metabolic enzyme, lowering levels of drugs it clears (e.g., carbamazepine causing contraceptive or warfarin failure).
When a drug blocks a metabolic enzyme, raising levels of drugs it clears and risking toxicity (e.g., fluvoxamine on clozapine).
A liver conjugation pathway (not CYP) that clears drugs like lorazepam; preserved in liver disease, making LOT benzodiazepines safer there.
Linear kinetics make drug levels dose-proportional and predictable; nonlinear/saturable absorption (gabapentin) means bioavailability falls as dose rises.
A drug that blocks a neurotransmitter transporter (e.g., SERT or NET), raising synaptic levels; methylphenidate blocks reuptake.
A drug that actively pushes neurotransmitter out of the neuron; amphetamines release dopamine and norepinephrine, unlike a reuptake blocker.
One of two mirror-image forms of a molecule; the active one may be marketed as a purified single-enantiomer drug (escitalopram from citalopram).
A reversible MAO-A inhibitor (moclobemide): tyramine can displace it, so no strict low-tyramine diet and a much shorter washout than irreversible MAOIs.
The strongest warning on a drug label, flagging serious or life-threatening risks.
Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy: a mandated safety program (e.g., monitoring, certification) required to prescribe or dispense a high-risk drug.
A list of medications (e.g., benzodiazepines, hypnotics) considered potentially inappropriate in older adults because of harm risk.
A dose above which a drug's effect plateaus; buprenorphine's ceiling on respiratory depression makes overdose safer than with methadone.
A long-acting injectable antipsychotic given every few weeks to maintain steady levels and support adherence.
'As needed' dosing — a medication taken only when symptoms occur rather than on a fixed schedule.
A symptom score for alcohol withdrawal used to guide symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing.
A steroid that modulates brain GABA-A signalling; neurosteroids (brexanolone, zuranolone) are used for postpartum depression.
A drug that depletes presynaptic dopamine by blocking the VMAT2 transporter; used to treat tardive dyskinesia and chorea (tetrabenazine, valbenazine).
A drug that blocks the glutamate NMDA receptor; underlies ketamine's rapid antidepressant effect and memantine's action in dementia.
A drug that turns down constitutive 5-HT2A receptor signalling; pimavanserin is a selective 5-HT2A inverse agonist for Parkinson psychosis.
A reduced response to a drug with repeated use, needing higher doses for the same effect; develops with benzodiazepines and opioids.
A state in which stopping a drug provokes withdrawal; develops with benzodiazepines, opioids and other agents.
A drug that fully activates a receptor (methadone at the mu-opioid receptor), with no ceiling on effects like respiratory depression.
The likelihood a medication is misused or diverted for non-medical use; high for stimulants and short-acting, high-potency benzodiazepines.
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor: first-line antidepressants and anxiolytics that raise serotonin (e.g., sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram).
Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (venlafaxine, duloxetine); its noradrenergic action can raise blood pressure at higher doses.
An older antidepressant class; effective but anticholinergic and, crucially, cardiotoxic in overdose (sodium-channel block, wide QRS, arrhythmia).
Monoamine oxidase inhibitor: potent older antidepressants needing a low-tyramine diet and washouts to avoid hypertensive crisis and serotonin syndrome.
GABA-A positive modulators (lorazepam, diazepam) for anxiety, seizures and sedation; cause tolerance, dependence and withdrawal — use short-term.
Older sedative-hypnotics (phenobarbital) with a narrow therapeutic index and lethal overdoses not reversed by flumazenil; largely obsolete.
Non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (zopiclone, zolpidem) acting at GABA-A; short-term sleep aids that can still cause dependence and complex sleep behaviours.
An 'atypical' antipsychotic with lower EPS than older agents but variable metabolic risk (weight, glucose, lipids).
A 'typical' antipsychotic (e.g., haloperidol): a potent dopamine (D2) blocker with high EPS and prolactin-elevating potential.
A first-generation antipsychotic family (e.g., chlorpromazine); low-potency members are sedating, hypotensive and anticholinergic.
A first-generation antipsychotic class (zuclopenthixol, flupenthixol) available in Canada, including as depots.
A high-potency first-generation antipsychotic class (haloperidol, droperidol) with strong D2 blockade and prominent EPS.
Norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor (bupropion): activating, weight-neutral and free of sexual dysfunction, but lowers the seizure threshold.
A selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (atomoxetine): a non-stimulant ADHD treatment without reward or misuse potential.
Drugs that block acetylcholinesterase to boost brain acetylcholine in Alzheimer disease (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine); cause GI upset and bradycardia.
A drug blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, urinary retention and, at excess, delirium.
An H1-receptor blocker; first-generation agents (hydroxyzine, diphenhydramine) are sedating and used PRN for anxiety or sleep, without dependence.
A beta-adrenergic antagonist; propranolol is first-line for akathisia and treats tremor, but non-selective agents can cause bronchospasm in asthma/COPD.
An alpha-1-adrenergic antagonist (prazosin) used off-label for PTSD nightmares; can cause first-dose orthostatic hypotension.
A drug stimulating central alpha-2 receptors to reduce sympathetic outflow (clonidine, guanfacine, dexmedetomidine); calms without causing EPS.
A drug promoting sleep onset and maintenance by blocking wake-promoting orexin, without GABA activity or dependence (lemborexant, daridorexant).
